coredata.dc:description: Eco-friendly, good water soluble and inexpensive, carbon dots (CDMU and CDMH) were synthesized, characterized and applied as corrosion inhibitor for mild steel (MS) in 15% HCl solution. The characterization of both inhibitors was carried out by FTIR, UV–vis, photoluminescence, XPS and TEM. The average size of CDMU and CDMH were found as 2.03 and 2.5 nm, respectively. The inhibitive effect of CDMU and CDMH was evaluated by weight loss measurement, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and potentiodynamic polarization measurement. Both CDMU and CDMH exhibited 97.89 and 90.10% of inhibition efficiency, respectively, at 100 mg/L concentration and 303 K temperature. Adsorption of CDMU and CDMH on MS surface followed Langmuir adsorption isotherm. The surface morphology of uninhibited and inhibited MS specimens was studied by the FESEM, AFM and XPS analysis.

head.ce:title: Novel carbon dots as efficient green corrosion inhibitor for mild steel in HCl solution: Electrochemical, gravimetric and XPS studies

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, IIT(ISM) Dhanbad, Jharkhand, 826004, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, IIT(ISM) Dhanbad, Jharkhand, 826004, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Eco-friendly, good water soluble and inexpensive, carbon dots (CDMU and CDMH) were synthesized, characterized and applied as corrosion inhibitor for mild steel (MS) in 15% HCl solution. The characterization of both inhibitors was carried out by FTIR, UV–vis, photoluminescence, XPS and TEM. The average size of CDMU and CDMH were found as 2.03 and 2.5 nm, respectively. The inhibitive effect of CDMU and CDMH was evaluated by weight loss measurement, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and potentiodynamic polarization measurement. Both CDMU and CDMH exhibited 97.89 and 90.10% of inhibition efficiency, respectively, at 100 mg/L concentration and 303 K temperature. Adsorption of CDMU and CDMH on MS surface followed Langmuir adsorption isotherm. The surface morphology of uninhibited and inhibited MS specimens was studied by the FESEM, AFM and XPS analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Mild steel is frequently used in several industrial sectors due to its numerous physical properties and inexpensiveness [1], but MS corrosion is one of the serious problems encountered by the industries. The corrosion causes, ample expenses for the reestablishment, renewal of various constitutes and equipment, life loss, and environmental problems [2]. Most of the industrial processes such as oil well acidization to increase the production of crude petroleum oil is carried out by pouring 15% HCl solution through steel tubes because it removes deposited salts and scales in flow channels. However, the aggressive nature of 15% HCl solution causes severe corrosion and damage to the steel, degrade its properties and limit the application of steel. Because of all these issues, researchers thought towards the corrosion problem and made plans to increase the functioning life of infrastructure, machines, metallic devices and so on. Degradation of various metals can be suppressed significantly by using various corrosion protection methods such as, improving the materials, applying inhibitors, alloying, using various types of coating, and by modifying the environment. Out of these methods, application of corrosion inhibitors is most simple and cost-effective method for corrosion protection. Corrosion inhibitors protect the materials from corrosion by adsorption on the metal surface and show outstanding anticorrosive activities [3]. The compounds framed with heteroatoms such as N, S, O and π electrons in their structure are reported as good corrosion inhibitor in acidic environment [4–10]. These types of molecules easily adsorbed on the surface of the metal by π and non-bonded electrons present in the moiety, thus decreases the contact area of the metal for interaction with the corrosive medium [11–14]. This results in a suppression of the corrosion by hindering the active sites of the surface. Corrosion inhibitors are widely used in various industries for preventing the corrosion in acidic environments. The major industries using corrosion inhibitors are the oil and gas exploration and production industry, the petroleum refining industry, the chemical industry, the heavy industrial manufacturing industry, the water treatment industry and the additive product industry. Presently, various organic compounds have been reported as corrosion inhibitor [15–19], but most of them are toxic in nature and effective at higher concentration. The increased awareness towards human health and environmental pollution has directed chemists to searching for the anticorrosive compounds having non-toxic, eco-friendly, safe and outstanding inhibition efficiency [20,21].

Carbon dots (CDs) have gained so much attention due to their excellent properties such as low toxicity, high-water solubility, unique photoluminescence (PL) and biocompatibility [22,23]. In general, carbon dots have applications in sensing, optoelectronic devices, cell imaging, biomedicine, catalysis and corrosion inhibition [4,24]. Ye.et al. prepared three types of N-CDs by hydrothermal process, first type N-CDs was prepared by taking citric acid, N-carboxysuccinimide and (1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride as raw material, second type N-CDs was prepared by using ammonium citrate as raw material and third type N-CDs was synthesized by taking methacrylic acid and n-butylamine as starting material and reported that these eco-friendly N-CDs displayed corrosion inhibition efficiency of 93.4, 84 and 94%, respectively, at 200 mg/L concentration and 303 K temperature for MS in 1 M HCl solution [4,25,26]. Yang et al. synthesized citric acid based functionalized ionic liquid CDs and studied its inhibitive nature on carbon steel in HCl and NaCl solution, got 92.6 and 83.45% efficiency, respectively, at 200 mg/L concentration [27]. Cen et al. prepared CDs by aminosalicylic acid and thiourea as initial material and reported 93% efficiency on carbon steel in NaCl solution at 50 mg/L concentration [28]. Cui et al. prepared CDs by 4- aminosalicylic acid as starting material and found its inhibition efficiency as 87.2% on steel in HCl at 100 mg/L concentration [29]. Wang et al. prepared N-doped CDs and calculated its inhibition effect on copper in H2SO4 solution, got 89.2% efficiency at 50 mg/L concentration [30]. Ye et al. synthesized functionalized carbon dots by taking l-histidine and citric acid as precursor and found 90% efficiency at 100 mg/L of concentration for Q235 steel in .1 M HCl solution [31]. Cui et al. synthesized N-doped CDs from p-phenylenediamine and o-phenylenediamine and examined its inhibitive nature on carbon steel in HCl, got 83.1 and 81.9% efficiency at 200 mg/L of concentration [32]. Cui et al. synthesized N-doped CDs from p-phenylenediamine and o-phenylenediamine and examined its inhibitive nature on carbon steel in HCl, got 83.1 and 81.9% efficiency at 200 mg/L concentration [33]. Li et al. prepared N-doped CDs by urotropine, EDTA and sulfamic acid as initial material and utilized as corrosion inhibitor for carbon steel in NaCl solution, found 83.7% efficiency at 600 mg/L concentration at 70 °C temperature [34]. We have already studied in our laboratory the inhibition efficiency of N, S-CDs and N-CDs for mild steel in hydrochloric solution and reported 96.4 and 90.0% inhibition efficiency, respectively, at 100 mg/L concentration and 303 K temperature [35]. Keeping in mind the good corrosion inhibition efficiency, eco-friendly nature and water solubility of carbon dots reported by other investigators as well as by us, we have planned to synthesize and characterize some new carbon dots having sufficient number of active sites and to study their corrosion inhibition efficiency for mild steel in 15% hydrochloric acid solution.

Therefore, two novel carbon dots, CDMU and CDMH, were synthesized using malononitrile, urea and 5-hydroxyisopthalic acid as starting material and used as corrosion inhibitor for MS in 15% HCl solution. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis), photoluminescence (PL), X-Ray photoelectronic spectroscopy (XPS) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were used to know the structure and composition of prepared inhibitors. Weight loss measurement (WL), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and potentiodynamic polarization measurement (PPM) were used to study the inhibitive effect of both carbon dots. The surface morphology of MS specimens was performed by using FESEM, AFM and XPS analysis.

The synthesis of CDMU and CDMH was accomplished by hydrothermal technique (Scheme 1 ). For CDMU equimolar mixture of malononitrile (0.198g, 3 mmol) and urea (0.18g, 3 mmol) were dissolved in 10 mL of water with sonication. Then, the dissolved mixture was transferred to autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. After completion of 8 h, mixture was cooled at room temperature. Then, the mixture was filtered and got desirable product. In case of CDMH whole procedure was same, but 5-hydroxyisopthalic acid was used in the place of urea. For CDMH, 5-hydroxyisopthalic acid (0.182g, 1 mmol) and malononitrile (0.18g, 3 mmol) were used. The resulting solution of both CDMU and CDMH were centrifuged for 10 min to remove larger aggregates, resulting in blue fluorescent carbon dots in pure conditions.

TEM images of studied carbon dots was performed by Technai F20 instrument. XPS analysis was performed using the 5000 versa probe ׀׀׀. FTIR spectra was captured by Agilent Cary 660 intrument. UV–vis spectroscopy and PL spectra of CDMU and CDMH was taken by UV- Agilent technologies Cary 8454 UV–vis and FI-Hitachi F-2700 fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively.

In all experiments, the MS specimens with configuration (wt%): C, 0.31; P, 0.010; Mn, 0.92; S, 0.008; Cr, 0.20; Si, 0.19 and Fe remainder were used. The MS specimens were cut in rectangular shape of size 6.0 × 2.5 × 0.1 cm and 1.0 × 1.0 × 0.1 cm for weight loss and electrochemical studies respectively. Prior to each experiment, MS specimens were handled mechanically with the help of polishing machine with several grade of emery papers ranging from 220 to 2000° sequentially. After polishing, cleaning by using distilled water and degreasing with acetone, specimens were dried prior to each use.
The test solution (15% HCl) was made by diluting concentrated HCl (37%) and the obtained solution was utilized for the formation of several dosage (15–100 ppm) of inhibitors at 303K.

Weight loss analysis was accomplished to evaluate corrosion inhibition efficiency (ƞ %), rate of corrosion (C R) and surface coverage (θ) according to ASTM method [36]. Each weight loss measurement was done in triplicate for the reproducibility of the experiment. MS specimens were immersed in 500 mL 15% HCl solution without and with several concentration of inhibitor for 6h and weight loss was measured. Using obtained weight loss data, C R, ƞ% and θ were evaluated by equations [37]:

Potentiodynamic polarization and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed with a regular three-electrode assembly in an electrochemical workstation CHI760D. Three electrode assembly consist of MS as working electrode with uncovered area 1 cm2, platinum as counter electrode and saturated calomel as reference electrode. All experiments were accomplished at 303 K. Prior to the each experiment, open circuit potential (OCP) was established by keeping the system for 30 min. Potentiodynamic polarization was performed in the potential range of −200 mV to +200 mV with respect to OCP. Afterwards, EIS was performed between frequency range 100 kHz–10 MHz at OCP with amplitude of 5 mV AC signal. For fitting the studied data Zsimpwin 3.21 software was used. Each potentiodynamic polarization and EIS experiments were done in triplicate for the reproducibility of the experiment.

FESEM micrographs of specimens were captured by HITACHI S3400 N instrument. For FESEM analysis, MS sample of dimension 1.0 cm × 1.0 cm × 0.1 cm were scraped away with several grade of emery papers, washed with doubly distilled water and acetone. The dried sampled were submerged in 15% HCl solution without with and CDMU and CDMH at 100 mg/L of dosage at 303 K for 6 h, after completion of 6 h samples were taken out, washed with doubly distilled water and acetone and dried then SEM micrographs were recorded.

AFM images were taken by the Agilent technology 5500 series instrument. For AFM analysis, MS samples of dimension 1.0 cm × 1.0 cm × 0.1 cm were abraded with several grade of emery papers washed with distilled water and dried. The dried samples were submerged in 15% HCl solution without and with CDMU and CDMH at 100 mg/L of concentration at 303 K for 6 h, after completion of 6 h, samples were taken out, washed with doubly distilled water and acetone and dried then AFM micrographs were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of synthesized CDMU and CDMH

ce:section.ce:para: TEM analysis was carried out to examine the morphology of CDMU and CDMH as shown in Fig. 1 . The morphology of CDMU and CDMH was unevenly spherical. The average size of CDMU and CDMH was found as 2.03 and 2.5 nm respectively. The success rate of the green synthesis hydrothermal approach in producing carbon dots was confirmed by HRTEM images, which were as displayed in Fig. 1.

We have synthesized CDMU and CDMH by hydrothermal technique. Both CDMU and CDMH showed blue colour emissive carbon dots. The fluorescent image of both carbon dots is showed in Scheme 1. To verify their fluorescent properties; we have checked their optical properties by UV–vis and PL spectroscopy. The CDMU showed UV absorbance (Fig. 2 (a)) peak at 223, 258, 295, 328 and 400 nm. The peak in the higher energy region (<300 nm) attributed to π-π* transitions of C-C/CC and the peak in lower energy region (>300 nm) assigned to n-π* transition of CN. The fluorescent image of CDMU is shown in Fig. 2 (b) and it showed the emission at 450 nm. On the other hand, Fig. 3 (a) of CDMH showed absorbance peak at 300 nm, which is assigned to the n-π* transition of CO. Fig. 3 (b) displayed the PL emission spectra. The CDMH showed excitation dependent wavelength emission spectra, where the maximum intense peak was appeared at 412 nm. It comprises that both CDMU and CDMH have high fluorescent properties.

FTIR spectra was performed to know the chemical structure of prepared CDMU and CDMH as shown in Fig. 4 (a) & (b). The spectrum ascribed several absorption bands, in case of CDMU, the broad peaks of absorption at 3343 cm−1 was assigned for O-H and N-H combined stretching vibration [34,38] and peak 2197 cm−1 [40] were ascribed to C≡N bond. The stretching vibration peak at 1637 cm−1 [34] and tensile vibration at 1400 cm−1 [39] was attributed to CN and C-N bond respectively. In case of CDMH, the stretching vibration peak at 3560 cm−1 [40] was assigned to O-H bond and the weak stretching vibration peak at 2207 cm−1 [39] ascribed to C≡N bond whereas peaks at 3180 (C-H stretching), 3050 (C-H stretching), 978 (C-H bending), 773 (C-H bending) and 1550 cm−1 (C-C stretching) assigned for the benzene ring. The strong absorption peak at 1696 cm−1 [41] was assigned to CO bond. Presence of these groups confirms the formation of CDMU and CDMH.

XPS analysis was accomplished to know the chemical composition and bonding in CDMU and CDMH. The surface survey of CDMU and CDMH showed the presence of C, N and O as displayed in Fig. 5 and their respective atomic percent was given in Table 1 . The high resolution deconvulated spectra of C1s for CDMU and CDMH are represented in Fig. 6 (a) and (b) respectively.

In spectra of CDMU and CDMH, four types of carbon bonding (Fig. 6(a) and (b)) corresponding to C-C/CC (284.5) [40,42], CN (285.6) [25], C≡N/C-N (286.6) [37,43], C-O (287.5) [44] and C-C/CC (284.0) [45], C-O (285.1) [46], C≡N (285.8) [46], CO (287.6) [47], respectively were observed. The N1s spectra (Fig. 7 (a)) of CDMU could be split in four peaks C≡N (397.4) [46], CN (398.0) [47], N-H (399.1) [29] and C-N (400.2) [48,49], on the other hand CDMH (Fig. 7(b)) showed single peak of C≡N (399.4) [49]. Meanwhile, O1s (Fig. 8 (a)) in CDMU of 531.9 eV [44] associated to the C-O and in case of CDMH ((Fig. 8 (b))) peak at 530.8 [11] and 532.0 eV [44] assigned to the CO and C-O respectively. XPS analysis outcomes were accordance with the FTIR spectra and further validate the formation of desirable compounds.

Weight loss analysis is one of the simple and reliable method for checking the inhibitor performance. This method was carried out by immersing MS specimen in 15% HCl solution at 15–100 mg/L of concentration of inhibitor for 6 h and 303–333 K temperature. Weight loss data as listed in Table 2 contains the value of inhibition efficiency (η %), corrosion rate (C R) and surface coverage (θ) of CDMU and CDMH at several concentration and temperature. The variation of C R and η % with inhibitor concentration at different temperature for CDMU and CDMH are displayed in Figs. 9 and 10
, respectively.

It can be observed from Table 2 and Figs. 9 and 10 that on addition of CDMU and CDMH in 15% HCl solution, C R of MS was significantly reduced, manifested the good efficiency of the synthesized carbon dots towards MS in HCl solution. On enhancing the concentration of inhibitors, inhibition efficiency also enhanced, shows the adsorption of CDMU and CDMH on MS surface and makes hindrance for mass and transfer of charge. This condition heading to the protection the MS surface by shielding active sites due to adsorbed molecules of inhibitors. The shielding effect is directly proportional to the surface coverage by the inhibitor molecules. As the inhibitor concentration enhances, the adsorbed number of molecules of CDMU and CDMH on MS surface enhanced.

The η % of CDMU and CDMH increasing with concentration up to 100 mg/L concentration and remains almost constant after this concentration. Therefore 100 mg/L was taken as optimum concentration for further study. The decrease in η % values with increase in temperature as displayed in Table 2 is due to the desorption phenomenon [50]. By increase in temperature adsorbed CDMU and CDMH molecules desorbed from the MS surface and as a result decrease in efficiency is noticed. The increase in temperature may also increase the mobility of CDMU and CDMH which is also responsible of lower inhibition efficiency.

The η % of CDMU and CDMH at 100 mg/L of concentration and 303 K temperature is 97.89 and 90.10 respectively. The starting material of both inhibitor CDMU and CDMH contains malononitrile. The inhibitor CDMU contains additional urea whereas CDMH contains additional 5-hydroxyisopthalic acid. Since the inhibition efficiency of an inhibitor is mostly dependent on number of atoms containing lone pair of electrons. Therefore, it can be concluded that the better inhibition efficiency of CDMU is because of the greater number of electron donor nitrogen atoms responsible for strong adsorption at metal surface as compare to CDMH.

The maximum corrosion inhibition efficiency (ƞ %) of CDMU and CDMH at 100 mg/L and 303 K temperature was found as 97.89 and 90.10 respectively. The maximum corrosion inhibition efficiency offered by the investigated CDMU inhibitor is higher as compared to the most of the carbon dots used as corrosion inhibitors and reported in literature [25–35]. On the basis of literature survey and best of our knowledge, the corrosion inhibition study of investigated CDMU and CDMH inhibitor has not been reported. Therefore the present investigation is novel and value addition in the area of design and development of effective water soluble eco-friendly corrosion inhibitors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of exposure time on corrosion inhibition performance of inhibitors
ce:section.ce:para: The corrosion inhibition performance of CDMU and CDMH was checked at different set of time (6–48 h) at 100 mg/L of concentration and 303 K and acquired data is displayed in Table 3 and Fig. 11 .

The corrosion rate and percentage inhibition with and without 100 mg/L of CDMU and CDMH in 15% HCl at several exposure periods. The corrosion rate was seen to increase on increasing the exposure time. The corrosion inhibition efficiency after 6 h of immersion time for CDMU and CDMH was 97.89 and 90.10% respectively and after 48 h it was reduced to 90.61 and 85.78% respectively. This reduction in efficiency is due to the steady desorption of CDMU and CDMH molecules from the protected MS surface in aggressive solution.

The activation and thermodynamic parameters were achieved from gravimetric analysis at concentration varying from 15 to 100 mg/L. The apparent activation energy (Ea) of metal dissolution was calculated by applying the Arrhenius equation:

In Fig. 12 log C R vs 1000/T plots were made and Ea was evaluated by the slope of plot at every temperature. The enthalpy of activation (ΔH a) and entropy of activation (ΔS a) values were determined by using transition state equation:

In Fig. 13 plot of log (C
R /T) vs 1000/T were formed and ΔH* and ΔS* values were evaluated by the slope and intercept respectively and the data is reflected in Table 4 . From Table 4 thermodynamic and activation data manifested that an increase in amount of inhibitor, increase the metal dissolution activation energy. It indicates that the corrosion process is slows down in presence of inhibitor. This result can be associated to adsorption of CDMU and CDMH molecules on the MS surface [51]. The ΔH* values were positive at all concentration of CDMU and CDMH. This observation illustrate that the transition state is formed by an endothermic complex formation process.

The positive values of ΔH* in case of blank as well as with inhibitors suggested that corrosion process is not affected by addition of inhibitors. The values of ΔS* were observed to be negative for both the inhibitors. This observation indicates towards association of the CDMU and CDMH molecules to the MS surface during the rate determining step. The values of ΔS a* were observed to increase with the increase in concentration of CDMU and CDMH. This observation indicated removal of more number of adsorbed water molecules from the experimental metal surface by adsorption of one molecule of CDMU and CDMH inhibitor.

To investigate the mode of adsorption of CDMU and CDMH, Langmuir adsorption isotherm (Fig. 14 ), Frumkin adsorption isotherm (Fig. 15 ) and Temkin adsorption isotherm (Fig. 16 ) were analysed. The adsorption parameters such as slopes and correlation coefficient of these adsorption isotherms are represented in Table 6
. It is observed from Table 6 that the values of correlation coefficient for Langmuir adsorption isotherm is maximum as compare to Frumkin and Temkin and approaches to unity. Therefore, the adsorption of CDMU and CDMH on MS surface obeyed Langmuir adsorption isotherm and the best fit was showed by Langmuir adsorption isotherm. Langmuir adsorption isotherm is displayed by the following equation:

Where c = concentration and K ads = equilibrium constant.

Fig. 14 displayed the relationship between C and C/θ. The values of slope and correlation coefficient (R2) obtained from Fig. 14 were nearly unity, indicated that Langmuir adsorption isotherm is followed. Table 5 displayed the values of K ads evaluated from the intercept of the plots C/θ vs C. The values of K ads for both inhibitors were higher, due to strong adsorption of CDMU and CDMH molecules on MS surface. From Table 5, CDMU has higher K ads values than CDMH manifested better adsorption of CDMU than CDMH. The free energy of adsorption

The potentiodynamic polarization analysis was performed to study the mechanism of anode and cathode reactions of corrosion. Fig. 17 describes the potentiodynamic polarization curves for MS in 15% HCl solution in presence and in absence of inhibitor. The corrosion current density (
i corr
), anodic and cathodic slope (βa, βc), corrosion potential (E corr) and corrosion inhibition efficiency (ƞ%) were measured. The corrosion current was obtained by Tafel extrapolation method. The ƞ% was evaluated by following formulae.
(8)
η % =
i corr
−
i corr 0
i corr

The corrosion parameters obtained from potentiodynamic polarization measurement are listed in Table 7 . To investigate the inhibitive behaviour of the studied carbon dots in-detail, potentiodynamic polarization analysis was carried out at different inhibitor concentration. It is apparent from Fig. 17 that the nature of polarization curves does not change, after addition of inhibitors in HCl solution, inferred that corrosion mechanism is not affected by addition of inhibitors. Both anodic and cathodic curves migrated towards lower values of corrosion current density, reports the mixed nature of both inhibitors. The values of βa and βc as reflected in Table 7 are varying after addition of inhibitors, indicating the change in anodic and cathodic current density which supports that both the corrosion inhibitors CDMU and CDMH act as mixed type inhibitors. From Table 7, it can be seen that the values of
i corr
reduced on increasing the concentration of inhibitor, inferred the adsorption of CDMU and CDMH on MS surface.

The difference between measured values of corrosion potential (E corr) in the presence of inhibitor and in absence of inhibitor are less than ±85 mV, inferred the mix nature of both inhibitors [53]. In presence of CDMU and CDMH, measured E corr values shifted slightly towards positive path as compare to the blank by which it can be adequately illustrated that both inhibitors act as a mix type inhibitor with predominantly anodic nature. The minor change in βa and βc values after addition of CDMU and CDMH supports that both inhibitors-controlled anode and cathode reaction. The
η %
values of CDMU and CDMH were ranging from 77.4 to 68.5% at 15 mg/L to 96.5% and 90.3% at 100 mg/L concentration, respectively. Thus, it can be stated that stable CDMU and CDMH adsorbed film is formed on the MS surface, thereby making a shield against the metal dissolution at the anodic site and the hydrogen evolution at the cathodic site in 15% HCl solution and reducing the value of anodic as well as cathodic current density.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used to investigate the behaviour of the metal/solution interface in the absence and presence of examined inhibitors. The Nyquist and Bode plots for MS in aggressive solution at 303 K, without and with several concentrations of CDMU and CDMH are depicted in Fig 18, 20 and 21 . These plots were obtained after 30 min of submersion of MS samples in 15% HCl solution without and with different concentration of CDMU and CDMH and obtained electrochemical parameters are given in Table 8 . The Nyquist plots of CDMU and CDMH show only one-time constant corresponding to one capacitive loop, recommended that the kinetics of the electrochemical system is limited by a charge-transfer control process. It is visible from Fig. 18 that the appearance of the curve before and after addition of inhibitors is same, declares that addition of CDMU and CDMH in 15% HCl solution do not alters the corrosion mechanism. Imperfect semicircle obtained in Nyquist plots with one-time constant is due to the rough surface of the MS electrode [54,55]. The capacitive loop diameter increases noticeably when CDMU and CDMH were added in the15% HCl solution. This regular increase in diameter on increasing the concentration declares the adsorption of studied inhibitor molecules on the surface of MS or establishment of thin film of studied carbon dots. This protective film decreases the active surface area of the MS, increases its corrosion resistance behaviour and notably inhibits the MS corrosion in 15% HCl solution [56]. The equivalent circuit, displayed in Fig. 19
was used for fitting the experimental data. This equivalent circuit (Fig. 19) contains solution resistance (Rs), polarization resistance (Rp) and the constant phase element (CPE). The CPE was used in equivalent circuit instead of double-layer capacitance (C dl) for good fitting of experimental data. Similar type of circuit has been also used by different researchers to study the corrosion inhibition of mild steel in hydrochloric acid solution [57–60]. The small values of χ2confirms the good fitting of Nyquist plots and approved it use of the equivalent circuit. The values of C dl, and ƞ% can be determined by the following equation:

It is evident from Fig. 18 that there is a good correlation between the theoretical data (dotted lines) and experimental data (hard liner), demonstrating that the equivalent circuit is shown in.

Fig. 19 is suitable for fitting the Nyquist plots. From the Nyquist plots different corrosion parameters such as solution resistance (Rs), polarization resistance (Rp), the constant phase element (CPE), Y 0 (CPE constant) and, n (CPE exponent) and inhibition efficiency (ƞ%) were determined as shown in Table 8. The value of n represents the deviation from the ideal behavior and it lies between 0 and 1 as confirmed by Table 8. The EIS parameters are displayed in Table 8. From EIS data, it can be seen that Rp values increases while C dl values decreases with increasing concentration of CDMU and CDMH. Thereby, it can be stated that CDMU and CDMH molecules adsorbed on MS surface by replacement of water molecules on metal interface, leading to the decrease in rate of metal dissolution. As a result, increase in ƞ% values with increase in concentration of CDMU and CDMH. The inhibition efficiency of CDMU and CDMH was found as 79.3% and 68.1%, respectively, at 15 mg/L concentration, and 97.9% and 91.8% for 100 mg/L concentration respectively at 303 K. This result confirms the adsorption of inhibitors on ms surface.

The Bode plots are represented in Figs. 20 and 21. The single peak was seen in Bode plots for both CDMU and CDMH revealed that the single time constant is present as stated in Nyquist plots. The increase in impedance magnitude over whole frequency range inceased by addition and enhancement in concentration of CDMU and CDMH. Also, the frequency range with the maximum phase angle increases as the concentration of studied carbon dots enhances. These results suggested the adsorption of CDMU and CDMH molecules on MS surface.

FTIR analysis was accomplished to know the chemical features of the thin film produced on MS surface after exposure to 100 mg/L of CDMU and CDMH in 15% HCl solution as depicted in Fig. 22 . The FTIR spectra of pure CDMU and CDMH is shown in Fig. 4. On comparing Figs. 4 and 22, we can see that the shifting of peak occurs after exposure in corrosive solution, showing the interaction of studied inhibitors and MS surface. In CDMU inhibited spectra, broader peak of O-H and N-H present in pure CDMU shifted to 3385 cm−1 and absorption peak of C≡N, CN and C-N were shifted to 2221, 1627 and 1402 respectively. In CDMH inhibited spectra, the absorption peak of O-H, C≡N and CO were shifted to 3360, 2215 and 1623 cm−1 respectively, whereas peaks corresponding to benzene ring shifted to 3190 (C-H stretching), 3065 (C-H stretching), 990 (C-H bending), 790 (C-H bending) and 1570 cm−1 (C-C stretching).

XPS analysis was conducted to validate the formation of the adsorbed layer and to study the composition of the inhibitor layer formed on the MS surface. Fig. 23 represents the surface survey spectra of CDMU and CDMH, which shows the presence of all the elements (C, N and O) present in the inhibitor as well as Fe and their respective atomic percent is given in Table 9 . The high resolution C1s spectra of CDMU and CDMH (Fig. 24 (a) and (b)) could be assigned to four peaks of C-C/CC (284.1) [45], CN (285.2), C≡N/C-N (286.2) [43], C-O (288.1) [43] and C-C/CC (284.3) [43], C-O (285.1) [61], C≡N (286.1) [43], CO (288.3) [62] respectively.

The high resolution O1s spectra for CDMU and CDMH (Fig. 25 (a) and (b)) could be assigned to two peaks each. The peak at 531.3 for CDMU and 531.5 for CDMH is ascribed to the OH− of FeOOH [63]. The peak at 532.1 [64] of CDMU and 532.7 [65] for CDMH ascribed to the surface adsorbed molecules of water.

The deconvulated N1s spectra of CDMU and CDMH (Fig. 26 (a) and (b)) could be ascribed into five peaks (three peaks for CDMU and two peaks for CDMH). For CDMU the peak at 398.9, 399.8 and 400.7 eV could be assigned to N-H [28], CN+ [67] and N-Fe [68] respectively. In case of CDMH the peak at 399.4 [56,66] and 400.9 [57,67] could be ascribed to CN+ and N-Fe respectively.

The high-resolution Fe 2p spectra (Fig. 27 (a) and (b)) is detected as doublet for both CDMU and CDMH where Fe 2p3/2 was seen at 710.8 eV for CDMU and 710.5 eV for CDMH; Fe 2p1/2 is seen at 723.8 eV for CDMU and 723.6 eV for CDMH. The Fe 2p3/2 is deconvoluted into further three peaks confirming the presence of Fe0 (708.9 eV, 708.5 eV), Fe2+ (710.8 eV, 710.5eV), Fe3+ (713.5 eV, 713.3 eV) and satellite of Fe3+ (716.4 eV, 716.5 eV) for both CDMU and CDMH respectively [65,68].
To get the detailed information about the changes occurred on MS surface during corrosion process with and without inhibitors, FESEM micrographs were taken (Fig. 28 ).

Fig. 28 (a) displayed the polished MS surface without any pits and cracks except some polished abrading scratches. Fig. 28 (b) displays the MS surface after dipping in aggressive solution without inhibitor shows highly corroded surface and presence of many cracks and pits because of severe corrosion; Hence MS surface is badly damaged in absence of inhibitors. Fig. 28 (c) and (d) displays MS surface after dipping in aggressive solution with CDMU and CDMH indicated comparatively smoother surface with lesser number of pits and cracks suggested the protective layer formation on MS facet. CDMU surface is smoother than CDMH, indicated that CDMU is better inhibitor than CDMH.
AFM analysis was performed to know the average roughness of MS surface before and after submerging MS sample in 15% HCl solution in absence and presence of inhibitor.

Fig. 29 (a) is the topography image of polished MS surface with average roughness of 96.8 nm. The corresponding height profiles of MS surface were displayed 29 (e-h), supports the results as lesser height of peak seen in case of polished and inhibited (CDMU and CDMH) sample. Fig. 29 (b) is the topography image of MS surface without inhibitor seems badly damaged because of attack of acid and its average roughness was 605.9 nm. Fig. 29 (c) and (d) are the topography images of MS surface in presence of CDMU and CDMH inhibitor, it is visible from these images that surface of MS becomes smooth with reduction in value of average roughness of 145.1 and 483.9 nm in case of CDMU and CDMH, respectively. The smaller value of average roughness in case of CDMU as compare to CDMH suggested better adsorption of CDMU as compare to CDMH on MS surface, Hence CDMU is better inhibitor than CDMH.

The inhibitor CDMU and CDMH get adsorbed on the surface of MS through heteratoms (nitrogen and oxygen) by donor-acceptor bonds (chemisorptions) and charge on CDMU and CDMH in acid solution also has a significant influence on the adsorption through electrostatic force of attraction (physisorption). The synthetized carbon dots possess several functional groups, such as hydroxyl, amino, cynide and carbonyl groups as confirmed by FTIR (Fig. 22) also supports the adsorption of inhibitor molecules on MS surface. The existence of N-Fe in XPS (Fig. 26) indicated that a part of the functional groups is distributed on the surface of carbon dots and caused strong adsorption on MS steel. The potentiodynamic polarization measurement (Fig. 17) shows that carbon dots (CDMU and CDMH) adsorbed on MS steel surface and prevent the metal dissolution process at anode and suppress the cathodic evolution of hydrogen. The presence of carbon dots blocking the active sites on the metal surface and inhibit the corrosion. The existence of functional groups on their surface may result in strong tendency for carbon dots to attract one another and produce enormous agglomerates (nanoparticles). Thus, the carbon dots nanoparticles adhered to the metal surface and formed the protective film which prevents the corrosion medium from the direct contact with mild steel. On the basis of UV–vis absorption of CDMU and CDMH (Figs. 2 and 3), we can see the peaks corresponding π-π*, n– π* and charge transfer transitions. These properties of CDMU and CDMH supports to enhance the adsorption process on the MS surface and results in improvement of η % of both inhibitors. Therefore, on the basis of these results we can conclude that corrosion inhibition of MS occurs through mixed adsorption of inhibitors (CDMU and CDMH) on MS surface.

CDMU and CDMH were found as very good water-soluble corrosion inhibitor for MS in 15% HCl solution. The studied carbon dots were synthesized by hydrothermal method and characterized by using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy, photoluminescence, X-Ray photoelectronic spectroscopy and Transmission electron microscopy. The average size of CDMU and CDMH were found 2.03 and 2.5 nm, respectively. The inhibition efficiency of CDMU and CDMH at 100 mg/L of concentration is 97.89 and 90.10% respectively at 303 K. Therefore, CDMU is better inhibitor than CDMH due to presence of higher number of active centres. The higher the concentration of CDMU and CDMH, more the protection ability due to strong adsorption and higher the temperature, reduction in protection ability due to desorption. Adsorption of CDMU and CDMH on MS surface obeyed Langmuir adsorption isotherm. Potentiodynamic measurement suggested that CDMU and CDMH are mixed type inhibitor and according to EIS, enhancement in charge transfer resistance with enhancement in concentration of CDMU and CDMH, although reduction in double layer capacitance with increase in concentration indicated the adsorption of studied inhibitor on MS surface. SEM and AFM micrographs validated the adsorption of CDMU and CDMH on surface of MS. Elemental composition of the adsorbed layer studied by XPS. Also confirms the adsorption of inhibitor molecules at the surface of MS.
The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared at this time due to technical or time limitations.
